description: Figure 2 presents the isothermal phase sections of Fe–Mn–C–Al low-density steels at 900 °C across three fixed Mn contents: a) 10 wt%, b) 20 wt%, and c) 30 wt%, with Al (wt%) on the abscissa and C (wt%) on the ordinate. The diagrams delineate single-phase and multiphase fields, where light gray, gold, and blue regions denote austenite (γ), ferrite (α), and κ-carbide, respectively; other labeled regions include γ+M₃C, γ+κ, γ+κ+α, α+κ, and B2+κ. As Mn increases from 10 wt% to 30 wt%, the austenite stability domain expands toward high-Al concentrations (>8 wt%), while the κ-phase field grows at high C (>3 wt%) and high Al (>10 wt%). Elevated Al concurrently promotes ferrite (α) formation, and C contents above approximately 1.2 wt% lead to M₃C and κ precipitation. Derived from CALPHAD calculations and experimental validation, these 900 °C isothermal sections are critical for industrial alloy design because this temperature is close to hot-rolling conditions, guiding composition selection for single-phase austenite or duplex microstructures.
